summary: A brief interview excerpt with Mary Midgley discussing her early life overshadowed by the Great War, her critique of the modern worship of science, and her rejection of behaviourist psychology's dismissal of the self.

principles.statement: We must avoid the worship of science and recognize its actual limits.
principles.rationale: People have developed a grotesquely exaggerated notion of what science is and what it can achieve, treating it as an infallible authority.
principles.source_quote: When I began to look at the way people talk about science, I realised that there is this grotesquely exaggerated notion of what it is and what it does

signature_quotes.text: The worship of science is really quite widely practised now.
signature_quotes.context: Discussing the philosophy that underpins science and what goes wrong when scientists lack a sense of philosophy or history.

signature_quotes.text: When I began to look at the way people talk about science, I realised that there is this grotesquely exaggerated notion of what it is and what it does
signature_quotes.context: Explaining what first attracted her attention to the philosophy of science.

anti_patterns.description: The worship of science and holding a grotesquely exaggerated notion of what it is and does.
anti_patterns.why_it_fails: It treats science as an infallible authority or religion rather than understanding its actual philosophical and historical context.
It wrongly suggests that psychology cannot study the self and must only focus on external 'facts in the world'.
